introduction.p: was a warning label. It showed that Stokes' theorem is simple, but not loose. The boundary must have the right orientation. Holes must be counted. Singularities cannot be ignored. The form must be smooth enough for d\omega to mean what we say it means.
Now we put the chapter in working order.

subsection.title: The one sentence, read slowly
subsection.p: The symbol M is the object with an inside. It may be an interval, a plane region, a surface, or a solid.
The symbol \partial M is its oriented boundary.
The form \omega lives on the boundary. The form d\omega lives inside.

The degree of \omega is always one less than the dimension of M:
\deg \omega=\dim M-1.
Then d\omega has degree
\deg(d\omega)=\dim M,
so it can be integrated over M.
That is why the dimensions match.

subsection.title: A final worked translation
subsection.p:
Let C be the unit circle in the plane z=0, oriented counterclockwise when viewed from above:
C:\quad x^2+y^2=1,\qquad z=0.
Take the 1-form
\omega=-y\,dx+x\,dy+z\,dz.

First compute the boundary integral directly. Parametrize C by
x=\cos t,\qquad y=\sin t,\qquad z=0, \qquad 0\le t\le 2\pi.
Then
dx=-\sin t\,dt, \qquad dy=\cos t\,dt, \qquad dz=0.
So
\begin{aligned} \omega \amp = -\sin t(-\sin t\,dt)+\cos t(\cos t\,dt)+0\\ \amp = (\sin^2 t+\cos^2 t)\,dt\\ \amp = dt. \end{aligned}
Therefore
\boxed{ \int_C\omega=2\pi. }

Now compute using Stokes' theorem. The exterior derivative is
\begin{aligned} d\omega \amp = d(-y\,dx)+d(x\,dy)+d(z\,dz)\\ \amp = -dy\wedge dx+dx\wedge dy+0\\ \amp = 2\,dx\wedge dy. \end{aligned}

Let D be the flat unit disk in the plane z=0, oriented upward. Its boundary is C. Then
\begin{aligned} \int_C\omega \amp = \int_D d\omega\\ \amp = \int_D 2\,dx\wedge dy\\ \amp = 2\operatorname{Area}(D)\\ \amp = 2\pi. \end{aligned}

But the flat disk was not special. Let S be the downward-opening paraboloid cap
z=1-x^2-y^2, \qquad x^2+y^2\le1,
oriented upward. Its boundary is the same circle C, with the same induced orientation. Stokes' theorem gives
\int_C\omega=\int_S d\omega.
Since
d\omega=2\,dx\wedge dy,
the surface integral measures twice the signed area of the shadow of S on the xy-plane. With upward orientation, that shadow is the unit disk with positive orientation. Hence
\boxed{ \int_S d\omega=2\pi. }

The flat disk and the curved cap give the same answer because the boundary is the same. Stokes' theorem says the integral of d\omega over a surface depends only on the boundary, as long as \omega is defined on the region swept between the surfaces.

subsection.title: The formulas to keep
subsection.p: The formulas below are not separate miracles. They are the same theorem in different degrees.

For a space 1-form,
\omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz,
\boxed{ \begin{aligned} d\omega \amp = (R_y-Q_z)\,dy\wedge dz\\ \amp \quad+ (P_z-R_x)\,dz\wedge dx\\ \amp \quad+ (Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy. \end{aligned} }
This is the flux 2-form of
\nabla\times(P,Q,R).

statement.p: What degree should \omega have if M is a surface?
What degree should \omega have if M is a solid?
Why is the boundary of an interval written \{b\}-\{a\} instead of just \{a,b\}?
For a positively oriented plane region, why is an inner boundary around a hole walked clockwise?
If a surface is oriented upward, how do you determine the positive direction of its boundary curve?
What does \partial(\partial M)=\emptyset mean for a solid box?
What does d^2=0 say in vector language for d(df)?
What does d^2=0 say in vector language for d(d(P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz))?
Why can a form with d\omega=0 still have a nonzero integral around a closed curve?
Why must Stokes' theorem require \omega to be defined on the inside region, not only on the boundary?

exercises.title: Skills: translating between vector calculus and forms

statement.p: Compute df for f(x,y,z)=x^2y+yz^3-\cos x.
Compute d\omega for the plane 1-form \omega=(x^2-y)\,dx+(xy+3y^2)\,dy.
Compute d\omega for the space 1-form \omega=yz\,dx+xz\,dy+xy\,dz. Identify the vector field whose flux form is d\omega.
Compute d\Phi for \Phi=x^2\,dy\wedge dz+y^2\,dz\wedge dx+z^2\,dx\wedge dy. Translate your answer into divergence language.
Let \mathbf F=(P,Q,R). Write the work 1-form of \mathbf F. Then write d\omega_{\mathbf F} and identify the vector field it represents.
Let \mathbf F=(P,Q,R). Write the flux 2-form of \mathbf F. Then write d\Phi_{\mathbf F} and identify its coefficient.
Let R be a positively oriented plane region. Translate \oint_{\partial R}\mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r into a 1-form integral.
Let E be a solid region. Translate \iint_{\partial E}\mathbf F\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}_{\mathrm{out}}\,dS into a 2-form integral.
Let I=[-2,5] with the usual orientation, and let f(x)=x^3-4x. Compute both sides of \int_{\partial I}f=\int_I df.
Let R=[0,3]\times[0,2] with positive orientation, and let \omega=(y^2+x)\,dx+x^2\,dy. Compute \int_{\partial R}\omega using Green's theorem.
Compute the same integral in Problem 2 directly by integrating along the four sides of the rectangle.
Let C be the positively oriented boundary of the triangle with vertices (0,0),\qquad (4,0),\qquad (0,3). Compute \int_C x\,dy. Explain why this integral gives the area of the triangle.
Let A=\{(x,y):1\le x^2+y^2\le9\} with the standard orientation. Compute \int_{\partial A}x\,dy. Be careful with the orientation of the inner boundary.
Let \omega=-\frac y2\,dx+\frac x2\,dy. Use Green's theorem to compute \int_{\partial R}\omega when R is the ellipse \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}\le1.
Let S be the upper hemisphere x^2+y^2+z^2=4,\qquad z\ge0, oriented upward. Its boundary is the circle x^2+y^2=4,\qquad z=0. Use Stokes' theorem to compute \int_{\partial S}(-y\,dx+x\,dy).
Let S be any smooth upward-oriented surface whose boundary is the circle x^2+y^2=1,\qquad z=0. Use Stokes' theorem to compute \int_S 3\,dx\wedge dy.
Let E be the box [0,1]\times[0,2]\times[0,3], and let \Phi=x\,dy\wedge dz+y\,dz\wedge dx+z\,dx\wedge dy. Compute \int_{\partial E}\Phi using the divergence theorem.
Compute the same integral in Problem 9 by adding the six face integrals.
Let B_a be the ball of radius a centered at the origin. Use the divergence theorem to compute the outward flux of \mathbf F=(x,y,z) through \partial B_a.
Let \mathbf F=(y,z,x). Compute the outward flux of \nabla\times\mathbf F through any closed surface \partial E, assuming \mathbf F is defined on a region containing E.
Let \alpha=\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}\,dx+\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\,dy. Compute \int_C\alpha where C is the unit circle oriented counterclockwise. Explain why this does not contradict Green's theorem.
Let \mathbf G=\frac{(x,y,z)}{(x^2+y^2+z^2)^{3/2}}. Compute its outward flux through the sphere x^2+y^2+z^2=a^2. Explain why the divergence theorem cannot be applied to the whole ball.
Let \omega=x\,dy on the square R=[0,1]\times[0,1]. Compute \int_{\partial R}\omega with positive orientation, and then compute the integral with the clockwise orientation. What changed?
Let S be the flat disk x^2+y^2\le1,\qquad z=0, oriented downward. What is the induced orientation on \partial S? Compute \int_{\partial S}(-y\,dx+x\,dy).
Let E be the spherical shell 1\le x^2+y^2+z^2\le4. Describe the induced orientations on the two boundary spheres.

Let \Psi=\frac{x}{r^3}\,dy\wedge dz+\frac{y}{r^3}\,dz\wedge dx+\frac{z}{r^3}\,dx\wedge dy, \qquad r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}. Use the shell in Problem 17 to explain why \int_{\partial E}\Psi=0, even though each boundary sphere has nonzero flux.
Suppose \omega=d\eta on a region containing a compact oriented surface S with no boundary. Prove that \int_S\omega=0.
Suppose \Phi=d\omega on a region containing a solid E. Prove that \int_{\partial E}\Phi=0. Translate this into a vector calculus statement.

exercises.title: Capstone project: derive the four classical theorems from one statement

introduction.p: The goal of this project is to start with \int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_M d\omega and recover the four classical theorems.
Write one page for each theorem. Each page should include:

statement.p: the object M,
its oriented boundary \partial M,
the form \omega,
the exterior derivative d\omega,
the forms version of the theorem,
the classical vector-calculus version, if there is one,
one warning about orientation, holes, or singularities.

conclusion.p: The project is not about memorizing four formulas. It is about showing that the four formulas are the same sentence with different nouns.

exercises.title: Capstone project: one boundary, three surfaces

introduction.p: Let C be the unit circle x^2+y^2=1,\qquad z=0, oriented counterclockwise when viewed from above. Let \omega=-y\,dx+x\,dy.

statement.p: Directly parametrize C.
Use the flat disk D:\quad x^2+y^2\le1,\qquad z=0, oriented upward.
Use the upper hemisphere S_1:\quad x^2+y^2+z^2=1,\qquad z\ge0, oriented outward.
Use the cone S_2:\quad z=1-\sqrt{x^2+y^2},\qquad x^2+y^2\le1, oriented upward.

conclusion.p: In your write-up, explain why the three surface computations agree. Then reverse the orientation of C and describe what changes.

exercises.title: Capstone project: find the hidden mistake

introduction.p: Each statement below is wrong or incomplete. Find the missing condition or sign error.

statement.p: Since d\left(\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}\,dx+\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\,dy\right)=0, the integral of this form around the unit circle must be 0.
The boundary of an annulus is only its outer circle.
If a disk is oriented upward, we may choose either clockwise or counterclockwise orientation on its boundary and Stokes' theorem will still hold.
Since the disk x^2+y^2\le1,\qquad z=0 has no volume, the divergence theorem says the flux of (0,0,1) through it is 0.
Since \nabla\cdot\left(\frac{(x,y,z)}{r^3}\right)=0 away from the origin, the outward flux through a sphere around the origin is 0.
A cube surface has edges, so as a surface it must have a boundary curve.
If \nabla\times\mathbf F=\mathbf 0, then every line integral of \mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r around a closed curve is 0, even if the domain has holes.
If two surfaces have the same boundary, then \int_S \Phi must be the same for every 2-form \Phi.

subsection.title: Where the theorem leads next
subsection.p: Stokes' theorem compares what happens on a boundary with what happens inside: \int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_M d\omega. That sentence is already a conservation law in disguise.

Imagine a pollutant spread through a fixed tank E. Let \rho(x,y,z,t) be its density at time t. The amount inside the tank is \int_E \rho\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. If pollutant flows out through the boundary, the amount inside decreases. Let \Phi_{\mathbf J}=J_1\,dy\wedge dz+J_2\,dz\wedge dx+J_3\,dx\wedge dy be the outward flux 2-form of the flux vector field \mathbf J=(J_1,J_2,J_3). Then the boundary loss is \int_{\partial E}\Phi_{\mathbf J}. Stokes' theorem turns that boundary flux into an interior integral:
\int_{\partial E}\Phi_{\mathbf J} = \int_E d\Phi_{\mathbf J} = \int_E (\nabla\cdot\mathbf J)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz.

figure.caption: A conservation law compares change inside a region with flux through its boundary.

subsection.p: The next chapter follows that idea. Stokes' theorem was a theorem about space: boundary versus interior. Conservation laws add time. A quantity changes inside a region only because it is created, destroyed, or carried through the boundary. The same boundary principle that unified calculus now becomes the language of fluids, heat, waves, and fields.
